title: Platt Scaling and Temperature Scaling
concept: Temperature scaling is a simple post-processing method that calibrates model confidence without changing accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main cause of overconfidence in deep neural networks?

options.text: Training to minimize Cross-Entropy loss combined with high network capacity.
Having a learning rate that is too high.

exercises.explain: Correct! Cross-Entropy loss pushes logits to extremes, creating overconfidence.

cards.title: Cooling down the hot soup
cards.body: If you are served a bowl of soup that is boiling hot, you can't eat it safely. You need to let it cool down. Similarly, when a network outputs logits that are too extreme, we need a 'temperature' parameter to cool them down before Softmax.

cards.title: Temperature Scaling
cards.body: Temperature Scaling is a post-processing technique where raw logits are divided by a single learned scalar T > 0 (the temperature) before applying Softmax: P = Softmax(Logits / T). T is optimized on a validation set.

cards.title: A volume knob on logits
cards.body: Think of T as a volume knob. If the model is overconfident, we turn the volume down (setting T > 1). This pushes the logits closer together, smoothing the Softmax output and lowering the predicted confidence.

cards.title: Temperature math
cards.body: If logits are [4.0, 2.0], standard Softmax gives [0.88, 0.12]. If we apply temperature scaling with T = 2.0, scaled logits become [2.0, 1.0]. Softmax of these is [0.73, 0.27]. Accuracy rank is identical, but confidence is calibrated.

cards.title: No effect on accuracy
cards.body: You might think dividing logits by a number changes the model's predictions and lowers accuracy. In reality, because dividing by T preserves the order of logits, the highest score stays the highest score. Accuracy remains exactly the same!

exercises.prompt: How does Temperature Scaling affect the final accuracy of a classification model?
exercises.explain: Dividing logits by a constant does not change which logit is the largest, meaning the chosen class (and accuracy) remains unchanged.

options.text: It has absolutely no effect on accuracy because it preserves the relative order of logits.
It decreases accuracy because it lowers the confidence scores.
It doubles the accuracy by correcting errors.

exercises.prompt: True or false: To calibrate an overconfident model, the learned temperature parameter T should be greater than 1.0.
exercises.explain: True. Setting T > 1.0 scales down extreme logits, bringing them closer to zero and softening the resulting probabilities.
Logits are divided by T, which is optimized using a validation dataset.
exercises.sentence: Temperature scaling divides raw ___ by a learned scalar T before Softmax.

exercises.prompt: Which parameter value of T will soften model predictions the most?
exercises.explain: A higher temperature value (T = 3.0) divides logits the most, bringing probabilities closest to a uniform distribution.

exercises.prompt: You are writing a post-processing calibration script. You have raw logits on a validation set. How do you find the optimal temperature T?

options.text: Run optimization (like L-BFGS) to find the value of T that minimizes Cross-Entropy loss on the validation logits.
Set T equal to the validation accuracy score.
Increase T manually by 0.1 every epoch during training.

exercises.explain: Exactly! You freeze the model weights, extract validation logits, and optimize the single parameter T to minimize validation loss. Once found, you apply this T to all future test/production logits. Next, let's look at how we measure calibration error!
